# Chapter 20

[k8r3m0v9] The flag went down and the line came apart like a chain snapping. Engines rose in pitch until the sound stopped being sound and became pressure in the chest. Flynn let the Charger roll half a breath and then fed it throttle and the tires bit and the hood lifted and the world narrowed to the strip of dirt ahead where the service road met the old highway. In the side glass he saw men flinch and grin with their hands over their ears. Dust climbed behind the cars in a brown curtain as if the ground itself was trying to keep them.

[0qf7n2cs] The first mile was chaos with a direction. Thirty cars converging on two lanes of broken pavement and no one willing to be the one who eased off. Metal noses darted. Mirrors flashed sun. Flynn kept his hands steady and his eyes far out, the way Diesel had drilled him, as if calm could be a tool you held. The Charger’s engine note was clean and hungry. It pulled like a thing that had been caged and suddenly remembered its own violence.

[t5a1xj2d] Cage’s Shelby found the front the way a shark finds blood. It threaded through openings that were not openings, inches to spare, its rear stepping out and then catching again. Flynn saw Birdie’s helmeted head in the passenger window for a second, jaw clenched, eyes too wide. Cage did not look left or right. His posture was loose, almost lazy, as if speed was the natural state and everything else was compromise.

[9m2c7v0h] They hit the highway proper and the numbers stopped meaning what they meant in cities. A hundred and twenty, a hundred and thirty, a hundred and forty. The road was sun-bleached and patched and it bucked under them in long shallow waves. The cars ran so close that Flynn could see heat shimmer off Mateo’s exhaust ahead and count the rivets on a spoiler to his right. Wind tore at the Charger’s weather stripping and whined through the gaps with a thin animal sound.

[1d6zq8p4] Flynn held eighth for a while without knowing how he’d gotten there. The pack stretched and compressed like lungs. Somebody’s bumper tapped his quarter panel and the Charger twitched and then settled. He tasted copper. He told himself it was from biting his tongue. The desert flared by on both sides, scrub and stone and occasional skeletal billboards still promising resorts that never got built. In his implant feed the speed warnings tried to overlay the windshield and he blinked them away until his eyes hurt.

[r2n8k4w1] The Twins came up on the left together like a matched pair of knives, their Camaros singing and sliding and showing off. They cut into a gap that was not there and the one nearest Flynn clipped the rear of a faded Nissan trying to defend its lane. It happened without drama, just a touch at a hundred and fifty and then physics finished the sentence. The Nissan yawed, tires smoking, and caught the other Twin’s nose. Both cars spun as if pulled by an invisible rope. One went sideways into the ditch. The other went end over end, bright paint flashing, and then the sound became a hollow boom and a bloom of orange.

[6s0v9j3k] Flynn saw the fireball in his mirror and forced his eyes back forward. No one slowed. The pack simply flowed around the wreck the way water flows around a rock, leaving it to burn on the shoulder. A helicopter peeled away from the sky like a black insect and dropped toward the smoke. The smell reached them seconds later, hot rubber and fuel and something sweet that made his throat close. Someone on the radio laughed once and then went quiet.

[qp4c1e7u] The highway bent toward the mountains and the air changed without cooling. The road began to climb. The shoulders narrowed. Guardrails appeared in pieces, broken and twisted, old wounds in steel. Checkpoint One was ahead somewhere, a gate in the climb, and the pack started to think again, each driver weighing risk against time, deciding what kind of person he was when the land fell away.

[7b9x3n6a] Cage treated the mountain like an insult. He shoved the Shelby into switchbacks too fast, brakes glowing in brief angry flashes, forcing slower cars wide until they rode the gravel at the edge and bounced back with their tires throwing stones. Flynn watched him do it to a low Corvette, pinning it toward the drop, and the Corvette’s driver lifted and fell back rather than die proving a point. Cage did not take the opening. He took the fear.

[cz1u4m8q] Flynn drove clean and fast, downshifting early, letting the Charger settle before each turn. He could feel the weight transfer through the seat and the steering column and it made sense in a way nothing else had lately. There was a rhythm to it. Turn in. Clip apex. Unwind. Throttle. The guardrail stuttered by on his left and on his right the mountain fell away into sun-washed emptiness. He thought of falling and felt his stomach go light and then the next corner arrived and the thought got cut off.

[5n8y0s2l] Diesel’s Chevelle appeared in his mirror, big and black, climbing through the field like it belonged here more than any of them. She took a line that looked wrong until it worked, using the whole road, touching dirt with one tire to keep speed. She passed Flynn on the inside of a hairpin with inches between their fenders. Her face was calm behind the visor. It was not bravado. It was competence.

[v4t7h9e0] Mateo was ahead, a dark shape that never wavered, his car eating corners without flourish. Flynn could not tell if the man was fearless or simply empty. The Mustang’s rear stayed planted, its movements small and perfect, like a machine following code. Yuki’s RX-7 flashed yellow farther up, light as a wasp, darting through gaps with a kind of elegant violence. Flynn chased them all and felt the Charger’s limits rising to meet him like a wall he could keep climbing.

[8h3p6d1r] On the next long left-hander there was a sharp report like a gunshot and Santos’s Impala jerked. The rear tire shredded and the car lurched toward the outside edge. Santos fought it, hands sawing, front wheels twitching, but the mountain did not care about experience. The Impala kissed the guardrail and the rail gave way with a sound like tearing cloth. For a second the car hung half on the road and half in air and Flynn saw Santos’s face through the window, calm and resigned as a man stepping off a porch.

[m0k2r7x9] Then the Impala went over. It did not tumble right away. It slid, nose down, slow as a boat leaving a dock, and then the cliff took it and the car became a small dark thing falling into glare. Flynn heard the thin distant crash a heartbeat later. The helicopter above them banked hard and dropped. The racers behind honked and swore and accelerated, as if speed could erase what they’d seen.

[j9v1s6q3] Checkpoint One came and went in a blur of banners and men with scanners. A kid leaned into the road with a handheld reader and the Charger’s tracker chirped and lit green and Flynn did not lift. The checkpoint was not a stop. It was proof you’d survived long enough to be counted. The road spilled down the other side into a wide valley and the pack stretched out, each car dragging its own private wake of heat.

[3w6c8t0n] The valley was a furnace laid flat. The sun hammered the windshield and the dash got too hot to touch. Mirage water pooled on the asphalt ahead and vanished as they reached it, the old trick repeated until it felt personal. Flynn’s mouth went dry. He sucked from the bladder tube and the water tasted like plastic and old pennies. His implant tried to warn him about hydration levels and he cursed it aloud and the word got stolen by the wind.

[fe7k2a4m] By the time the desert checkpoint appeared—just tents and fuel drums and a row of men with stopwatches—five cars were gone. Not behind. Gone. Empty stretches of road where an engine should have been. Flynn rolled in hard on the brakes and the Charger shuddered and settled in a square of shade that smelled of spilled gasoline. A man in a Don shirt jammed a nozzle into his filler neck while another slapped a scanner to his windshield. Sixty seconds. That was the mercy.

[2p0n7l5v] Cage hit the checkpoint like a bullet finding bone. The Shelby skidded sideways, tires scrabbling, and stopped so close to the drums that the front splitter kissed a dented barrel. Birdie climbed out on shaking legs and bent at the waist and retched nothing. Cage stayed seated, hands on the wheel, eyes forward as if he was still moving. Men poured fuel. Someone tightened a cap. Cage’s face did not change. It was the face of a man doing exactly what he was made to do.

[sd6y3k8b] Flynn saw Birdie’s eyes when Birdie looked up. They were wet and bright and wild. Terror and joy tangled together until they were the same thing. Birdie laughed once, breathless, and then swallowed it when Cage finally turned his head a fraction. Cage said something Flynn could not hear and Birdie nodded hard, eager, like agreement was the only way to stay alive.

[x1c9m4u7] Diesel pulled in beside Flynn, her Chevelle ticking with heat, her hands moving fast and sure. She leaned across the gap between their cars while a man filled her tank. “Two dead already,” she said, as if naming weather. “They say the revival bird couldn’t get there in time.” Flynn stared at her, trying to make death into a fact instead of a door. Diesel’s jaw flexed. She glanced toward Cage and the Shelby. “He’s going to kill someone,” she said. Flynn felt the truth of it settle in him like sand. “I know,” he said.

[4u2e8h6t] They launched again into dirt roads that turned the world into powder. The field was thinner now and the gaps between cars opened and then filled with dust that erased distance. Flynn followed a pair of taillights until they vanished in brown and then he was alone with the engine’s roar and the rattle of stones against the undercarriage. Far ahead the sky took on a dirty bruise color, a wall building itself out of wind. Checkpoint Three waited somewhere past it, a number on a map, a promise of halfway, and he drove toward it like you drive toward a storm because there is no other direction.
